headline: A holiday-weekend lull with the tape frozen at last week's closes — but Iran's Strait of Hormuz toll threat leaves oil (and India) the thing to watch, while Bitcoin quietly firms.
market_story: Today is a market holiday: US stock and bond markets are shut for July 4 (and the weekend), and India's exchanges are closed on Saturday — so the numbers below are frozen at last week's closes, not fresh moves. The week itself ended on a clear theme: a weak US June jobs report (just 57,000 jobs added, about half the ~115,000 expected) killed the fear that the Federal Reserve would RAISE interest rates. 'Cheaper money for longer' is good for stocks and gold, so the blue-chip Dow Jones closed at a record ~52,900 and gold sits near ~$4,187, while money 'rotated' OUT of expensive AI-chip names (Nasdaq -0.8%, Nvidia weak) into old-economy value. Over the long weekend two live threads matter. First, crypto — the only market open — where Bitcoin firmed to ~$62,795, clawing back above its short-term averages for the first time in weeks (an early stabilization sign, though still in a downtrend). Second, and bigger, is the OIL wildcard: a bruised, post-war Iran wants to charge tolls in the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow sea gate that carries about a fifth of the world's oil — and tankers were seen U-turning at its mouth, while Ukraine struck a major Russian oil terminal. Both threaten supply. Crude is technically oversold near ~$68.78, so any confirmed disruption could snap it higher when trading resumes Monday. For India that is THE risk: it imports ~85% of its crude and its rupee is near a record-low ~95.2 per dollar, so an oil spike would hit fuel prices, the currency and inflation — just as India's own demand is already cooling (services activity at a 17-month low).

board.name: S&P 500
board.read: The broad US large-cap gauge finished last week flat, its engine split between rising value stocks and falling tech. Frozen here — US markets are shut for the July 4 holiday and weekend.

board.name: Nasdaq
board.read: The tech-heavy index closed the week as the laggard, hit by money rotating out of pricey AI-chip stocks.

board.name: Dow Jones
board.read: Old-economy blue-chips at a record, the winners of fading rate-hike fears. RSI ~70 flags it as short-term 'overbought' (stretched after a fast run).

board.name: Nifty 50
board.read: India's benchmark edged up on domestic buying at Friday's close; exchanges are shut Saturday. Local buyers are still offsetting heavy foreign selling.

board.name: Sensex
board.read: India's 30-stock index tracked the Nifty higher — a modest, headline-driven up-move.

board.name: Bank Nifty
board.read: Banks dipped slightly and lagged the broader market — financials weren't the week's leaders.

board.name: Bitcoin
board.read: The only market open this weekend, and it firmed — up from ~$61,651 Thursday, reclaiming its short-term averages. An early stabilization sign, but still below its 50- and 200-day averages (a downtrend).

board.name: Ether
board.read: Ethereum firmed alongside Bitcoin (+1.9%) but remains technically weak, below both key moving averages.

board.name: Gold
board.read: Near its recent highs as fading rate-hike fears make the no-yield metal more attractive; a classic beneficiary of a softer-Fed backdrop.

board.name: Crude Oil (WTI)
board.read: Flat and technically oversold (RSI ~29) — but the live wildcard: a Strait of Hormuz disruption could spike it Monday. Steady sub-$70 oil is a quiet positive for oil-importer India, a spike the opposite.

board.name: USD / INR
board.read: The rupee is pinned near a record-low ~95.2 per dollar by foreign outflows and importer dollar demand; an oil spike would pressure it further, a softer Fed could eventually help.

themes.note: Iran's Hormuz toll threat + tankers U-turning + Ukraine hitting a Russian oil terminal make crude the biggest live wildcard into Monday. WTI is oversold at ~$68.78, so the risk is skewed to an upside spike — the key swing factor for India.
Weak June jobs (57k) buried the July rate-hike fear — the standing tailwind that put the Dow at a record and firmed gold.
Nasdaq -0.8% as money rotated out of stretched AI valuations; Nvidia is weak and Meta's 8,000 layoffs plus a 'progress slower than expected' admission add to the sentiment drag.

Bitcoin reclaimed its 10-, 20- and 30-day averages over the long weekend with MACD turning positive — early stabilization, though still below the 50- and 200-day averages that define the bigger downtrend.
Indices firm on domestic buying, but services PMI at a 17-month low, record foreign outflows and a rupee near 95.2 keep the currency and imported-inflation risk live.

what_to_watch.title: The Strait of Hormuz oil wildcard
what_to_watch.gist: A weakened, post-war Iran says it wants to charge tolls for ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow channel that carries about a fifth of the world's seaborne oil — and on Saturday ship-tracking data showed tankers U-turning at its entrance. Separately, Ukraine struck a major Russian oil terminal. Crude (WTI) sits oversold near $68.78, meaning it has fallen hard and could snap back quickly on any real supply scare.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: There is no easy detour around Hormuz, so even the threat of a toll or blockage lifts shipping-insurance costs and can push oil up worldwide. Oil feeds straight into petrol, transport and food prices — and into inflation, which is exactly what a calmer oil market had been helping to tame.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: This is the single biggest thing that could move your book Monday. For India especially — an importer of ~85% of its crude with a record-weak rupee — a spike would hurt fuel costs, the currency and inflation, and pressure oil-sensitive Indian stocks; oil producers and energy names would be the relative winners.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether tankers keep avoiding Hormuz, and where Brent/WTI crude open when trading resumes Monday.

key_terms.definition: A narrow shipping lane between Iran and Oman through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil passes; there is no easy alternative route.
A momentum reading (RSI below ~30) suggesting a price has fallen fast and may be primed for a sharp bounce — though 'oversold' can persist.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: The oil price is set as much by the safety of the shipping ROUTE as by how much oil exists — a threat to one narrow strait can move global energy costs before a single barrel is lost.

what_to_watch.title: Weak US jobs killed the rate-hike fear
what_to_watch.gist: The US added just 57,000 jobs in June, roughly half the ~115,000 expected. The jobless rate fell to 4.2%, but mainly because people stopped looking for work — labour-force participation hit a 50-year low outside the Covid era. A cooling job market gives the Fed far less reason to raise interest rates.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: For months markets feared rate HIKES because of oil-driven inflation. Weak jobs flip that: the odds of a July hike collapsed. Since stocks, gold and crypto all suffer when rates rise, removing that threat is a broad tailwind — it is why the Dow hit a record and gold firmed.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: The immediate worry — higher borrowing costs — has eased, which supports your equity and gold exposure. But softer jobs also hint the economy is slowing, so it is relief with a caveat, not an all-clear.
what_to_watch.watch: The next US inflation (CPI) print and the July 29 Fed meeting — whether 'no hike' turns into talk of cuts.

key_terms.definition: The monthly count of US jobs added outside farming — the most-watched gauge of how healthy hiring is.
The share of adults working or job-hunting; when it falls, a lower jobless rate can be misleading because people simply gave up looking.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: Markets trade the future, not the present — 'bad' news for the economy can be 'good' news for stocks when it removes the threat of higher interest rates.

what_to_watch.title: Bitcoin stabilizes over the long weekend
what_to_watch.gist: Crypto is the only market that trades on a holiday weekend, and Bitcoin used it to firm: it rose to ~$62,795 from ~$61,651 on Thursday, and — importantly — climbed back above its 10-, 20- and 30-day moving averages (the average prices over those recent windows). Its momentum gauge (MACD) has turned positive. But it is still below its 50-day (~$67,039) and 200-day (~$74,825) averages and sits in just the 8th percentile of its 52-week range.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Reclaiming the short-term averages is the first thing that has to happen before any real turn — it shows sellers easing up. But a downtrend is not broken until price reclaims the longer 50- and 200-day averages, and Bitcoin's trend strength is still strong and pointing down, so this could just be a pause in a bigger slide.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For a Bitcoin holder this is an encouraging wobble, not an all-clear. Treat the ~$67k 50-day average as the level that would signal a genuine trend change; a slip back below ~$62.4k support would say the bounce has failed.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether Bitcoin can hold above ~$62.4k support and then challenge its 50-day average near ~$67,000.

key_terms.definition: The average closing price over a set number of days; price above it is a bullish sign for that time frame, below it bearish.
A momentum indicator; when its line crosses above its signal line, short-term momentum is improving.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: Crypto trades 24/7, so its story often moves on the weekend while stocks sleep — and a downtrend isn't over until the longer moving averages are reclaimed, not just the short ones.

what_to_watch.title: India's demand cools — services activity hits a 17-month low
what_to_watch.gist: India's services PMI — a monthly business survey where above 50 means growth — fell to a 17-month low of 57.4 in June as demand softened, particularly at home. It is still expanding, but the momentum is clearly fading. Meanwhile GST (sales-tax) collections rose 14% to ₹1.95 lakh crore, though partly on imported inflation rather than strong domestic buying.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Services are the engine of India's growth, so a multi-month low is the first real sign the domestic consumer is slowing. Layered on top are record foreign-investor outflows and a rupee near 95.2 — a mix that caps how far Indian indices can run and keeps imported-inflation risk alive, especially if oil spikes.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For an Indian investor the index strength is real but fragile — it rests on local buying while foreigners exit and demand cools. It argues for leaning toward quality and being wary of consumption names most exposed to a spending slowdown.
what_to_watch.watch: India's next CPI inflation print and the monsoon's progress (an El Niño-weakened monsoon would lift food prices and hit rural demand).

key_terms.definition: A monthly survey of businesses; above 50 signals expansion, below 50 contraction — and the trend matters as much as the level.
Foreign funds selling Indian shares and taking the money out; it pressures both the market and the rupee.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: A reading above 50 still means growth — but a multi-month LOW says the momentum is fading, and momentum is what markets pay for.

what_to_watch.title: The AI-chip rotation and Meta's reset
what_to_watch.gist: Last week money rotated OUT of expensive AI-chip names into cheaper value stocks: the Nasdaq fell 0.8% while the Dow hit a record. Nvidia is caught between its 50-day (~$210) and 200-day (~$191) averages, rated a short-term Strong Sell. Now Meta has laid off ~8,000 staff and CEO Zuckerberg has admitted AI-agent progress was slower than expected — a fresh crack in the AI story.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: So much of the US market's gains rested on a handful of pricey AI names. When rate fears fade AND the AI narrative wobbles, cheaper value stocks suddenly look more attractive than richly-priced growth, so leadership rotates. A record in the Dow alongside a falling Nasdaq is a sign of narrow, selective leadership — not a market-wide boom.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: If you own AI-chip names like Nvidia, expect choppier, two-way action while this rotation and the AI-expectations reset play out. It is a change in WHO leads, not necessarily a crash.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether Nvidia holds its ~$191 200-day-average support, and its next earnings/AI-capex guidance as the real test of the AI-spending thesis.

key_terms.definition: Investors moving money from one part of the market (e.g. AI tech) into another (e.g. value/old-economy), which shifts what leads.
The average price over the last 50 or 200 days; being caught between them signals a trend that could break either way.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: Even a powerful theme reprices when leadership rotates and expectations reset — the most richly-priced leaders are the first to feel it.

global_summary.us: US markets are closed for the July 4 holiday and the weekend, so levels reflect last week's close: S&P 500 ~7,483 (flat), Dow +1.14% to a record ~52,900, Nasdaq -0.8% (~25,833). The week's driver was a weak June jobs report that killed rate-hike fears, sparking a rotation out of AI chips (Nvidia weak) into value; Meta's 8,000 layoffs added to the AI wobble.
global_summary.india: Indian exchanges are shut Saturday; Friday closed firm on domestic buying — Nifty +0.39% (~24,271), Sensex +0.34% (~77,764), Bank Nifty -0.16% (~57,939). But services activity hit a 17-month low, foreign investors keep selling, and the rupee is pinned near a record-low ~95.2/$.
global_summary.crypto: The only market open this weekend, and it firmed: Bitcoin ~$62,795 (up from ~$61,651 Thursday), reclaiming its short-term averages; Ether ~$1,790 (+1.9%). Both are still technically in downtrends below their 50- and 200-day averages.
global_summary.macro: Fading US rate-hike bets keep gold firm (~$4,187) and support a softer dollar. The live risk is oil: crude sits oversold near $68.78, but Iran's Strait of Hormuz toll threat (tankers U-turning) and Ukraine's strike on a Russian oil terminal could spike it Monday. Next big date: the July 29 Fed decision.

catalysts_today.event: US stock and bond markets CLOSED for Independence Day (Jul 4 falls on Saturday; holiday weekend)
Iran's dayslong state funeral for the late Supreme Leader Khamenei — a Middle East / oil-risk backdrop
First market reaction to the Strait of Hormuz / oil news as US and India resume trading
US Federal Reserve rate decision — the read on whether 'no hike' hardens into cuts

favorites.name: Apple Inc.
favorites.whats_new: Frozen at Thursday's ~$308.63 close — US markets were shut Friday for July 4 and stay closed through the weekend, so nothing new traded. Apple is still sitting just below its $315 record after Thursday's ~4.8% jump on foldable-iPhone and cheaper-memory-chip reports, firmly in an uptrend.

news.headline: Apple's stock recovers after hit from unprecedented price hikes on products

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap tech, ~$4.53T market cap. Trailing P/E ~37 (expensive — you're paying up for growth), forward P/E ~32 (profits expected to grow). Revenue growing ~17% a year, keeping ~27¢ of every sales dollar as profit. Beta ~1.10 (about as jumpy as the market).
favorites.technicals: Strong Buy. Price ($308.63) sits above both its 50-day (~$294) and 200-day (~$271) averages — the classic shape of a healthy uptrend — and all 10 moving averages are bullish, near the top (94th percentile) of its 52-week range. RSI ~60 is neutral (not yet stretched), but Stochastic and Williams %R flag short-term 'overbought', so a pause or dip near the $314-315 highs wouldn't surprise. Support to hold: ~$298; a close below the 50-day (~$294) would warn the trend is stalling.
favorites.catalysts: Confirmation of foldable-iPhone orders and memory-chip deals; next quarterly earnings and AI/services commentary.
favorites.risks: Rich valuation leaves little room for a miss; trading near record highs on optimistic reports means any disappointment could sting, and a broader AI-tech pullback could still drag it.

favorites.name: Nvidia
favorites.whats_new: Unchanged over the holiday at ~$194.83 with US markets shut — still in the AI-chip penalty box after last week's rotation out of expensive tech. It remains caught between its 50-day (~$210) and 200-day (~$191) averages, and Meta's 8,000 layoffs plus Zuckerberg's 'AI progress slower than expected' admission are a fresh sentiment overhang for the whole AI complex.

news.headline: After laying off 8,000, Meta CEO Zuckerberg admits AI progress was slower than expected
Why Nebius & CoreWeave stocks tumbled after Meta's AI cloud plans

favorites.fundamentals: ~$4.72T market cap with exceptional ~63¢-per-dollar profit margins. Trailing P/E ~30, but forward P/E just ~15 — meaning the market expects profits to keep growing very fast (revenue ~85% a year). Beta ~2.21, so it swings roughly twice as hard as the market.
favorites.technicals: Strong Sell (short-term). Price ($194.83) is below its 50-day average (~$210) but above its 200-day (~$191), so the trend is transitional — it could break either way. MACD momentum is negative and 8 of 10 moving averages are bearish. Line in the sand: the ~$191 200-day-average support must hold; a close back above ~$210 would be the first sign the pullback is turning up.
favorites.catalysts: Next earnings and cloud-AI capex guidance — the real test of whether the AI-spending boom continues.
favorites.risks: A high-beta momentum name: sharp drawdowns if AI-valuation doubts deepen or the chip rotation continues; Meta's AI reset adds to the overhang.

favorites.name: Reliance Industries
favorites.whats_new: Essentially flat at ₹1,304 with Indian markets shut Saturday. The relevant weekend backdrop is supportive of its core: PM Modi vowed India will keep expanding oil-refining capacity for 'supply chain security', a tailwind for Reliance's oil-to-chemicals business — though the stock is still below both key moving averages, so no trend change yet. (Note: this week's CBI arrest of a former Reliance Capital CFO concerns Anil Ambani's separate Reliance ADA Group, not Mukesh Ambani's Reliance Industries.)

news.headline: Modi says India will keep expanding oil refining capacity
Petrol, diesel prices to fall only after cheaper crude reaches Indian refiners: Hardeep Puri

favorites.fundamentals: ~₹17.65T market cap, energy-heavy conglomerate. Trailing P/E ~22 / forward ~18 (moderate valuation), revenue growing ~12% a year, keeping ~8¢ of every sales dollar as profit. Beta ~0.18 — much steadier than the market.
favorites.technicals: Strong Sell. Price (₹1,304) sits below both its 50-day (~₹1,343) and 200-day (~₹1,419) averages — a textbook downtrend — with 9 of 10 moving averages bearish. But trend strength is weak (ADX ~16), so it's more of a drift than a plunge, and it's near the low end of its 52-week range (14th percentile), where value hunters watch; MACD has ticked positive. Needs to reclaim ~₹1,343 to shift the near-term picture; support ~₹1,300.
favorites.catalysts: Energy-refining margins (India's refining-expansion push and steady cheap crude help its oil-to-chemicals business), plus Jio/Retail updates.
favorites.risks: Below both moving averages; a soft rupee and the fate of energy/refining margins are the key swing factors — and a Hormuz-driven crude spike would raise its feedstock costs.

favorites.name: Bitcoin
favorites.whats_new: The one favorite that actually moved this weekend. Bitcoin firmed to ~$62,795, up from ~$61,651 Thursday, clawing back above its 10-, 20- and 30-day averages — its best footing in weeks and an early stabilization sign. But it is still below its 50-day (~$67,039) and 200-day (~$74,825) averages and sits in the 8th percentile of its 52-week range, so the downtrend isn't broken yet.
favorites.fundamentals: ~$1.26T total value; near the low end (8th percentile) of its 52-week range after a long slide from ~$114k.
favorites.technicals: Neutral (improving from Sell). Price (~$62,795) is below both its 50-day (~$67,039) and 200-day (~$74,825) averages — a confirmed downtrend — and trend strength is strong (ADX ~33), so down-moves here tend to persist. The bright spots: it reclaimed its short-term averages and MACD momentum has turned positive. First support to hold: ~$62,439; first resistance: ~$63,003. A close back above the 50-day (~$67k) is what would signal a real turn.
favorites.catalysts: Risk appetite and fund flows; a calmer Fed helps risk assets generally, but crypto needs to reclaim its longer averages to confirm a turn.
favorites.risks: Downtrend still intact despite the bounce; a slip back below ~$62.4k support could reopen the door toward fresh lows.

risks: Oil is the live wildcard: Iran's Strait of Hormuz toll threat (tankers U-turning) plus Ukraine's strike on a Russian oil terminal could spike oversold crude Monday, hitting oil-importer India's fuel bill, rupee and inflation.
The 'no-hike' relief is conditional: a hot US inflation (CPI) print before the July 29 Fed meeting could quickly revive rate-hike fears and unwind the Dow's record and gold's gains.
AI-chip rotation and reset: if doubts about stretched AI valuations deepen — amplified by Meta's 8,000 layoffs — Nvidia, the chip complex and the wider Nasdaq could stay under pressure.

India's twin drag: cooling domestic demand (services PMI at a 17-month low), record foreign outflows and a rupee near 95.2 cap gains and raise imported-inflation risk, with El Niño a monsoon/food-price wildcard.
Crypto is still in a downtrend despite the weekend bounce; a break below ~$62.4k support would signal the stabilization has failed and dent risk sentiment.
disclaimer: For information only. Not financial advice. Prices are from free, possibly-delayed sources (yfinance/CoinGecko) and can be inaccurate; markets were closed for the holiday weekend, so equity levels reflect last week's close. Verify before acting.
